David's victory over Rabbah arrives quickly in these verses, almost casually—but the swiftness conceals something unsettling. While Joab conducts the actual siege, David stays in Jerusalem. He takes the spoils and the crown, placing it on his own head as though the conquest were his alone. This is the same David who will soon fall into adultery with Bathsheba in 2 Samuel 11, a sin tied directly to his presence in the city when he should have been at war. The text doesn't condemn him here, but it marks the moment: a king absent from his duty, claiming victory he did not win, adorning himself with treasures extracted through conquest. The putting of people to work "with saws, iron picks, and axes" carries brutal undertones—not slavery exactly in the modern sense, but a subjugation that strips the defeated of their humanity. This is what kingship looks like when divorced from righteousness.

Yet the chapter pivots away from David's glory to something far stranger. A series of warriors emerges—Sibbecai, Elhanan, Jonathan, others whose names are barely preserved in Scripture's memory. They do what David cannot: they personally face and kill the giants, the Rephaim, those monstrous descendants of Rapha who tower over ordinary men. Elhanan kills Lahmi, the brother of Goliath. Another warrior takes down a man with twenty-four digits—six fingers and six toes on each hand and foot—whose very existence seems an affront to God's order. These aren't David's victories. These are the victories of men whose faithfulness goes unsung, whose courage receives no crown, whose names appear once and then vanish from history's record.

The contrast is deliberately constructed. David the king remains in the capital, consuming glory. His servants do the dangerous work, facing down the impossible. The giants fall not because of David's fame or anointing, but because ordinary soldiers trust God enough to stand. And yet we remember David's name, David's crown on his head, David's great amount of plunder. We have forgotten Sibbecai entirely. This should trouble us: What does it mean that Scripture preserves the record of obscure faithfulness only to have history forget it? The chapter asks silently whether the glory we claim and the glory we deserve are ever the same thing. The giants may be dead, but the question of true greatness—whether it belongs to the king in his palace or the soldier at the gate—lingers unanswered and uneasy.
